title: Geometric Vertex Decomposition
speaker: Patricia J. Klein
abstract:
Vertex decomposition, introduced by Provan and Billera in 1980, is an inductive strategy for breaking down and understanding simplicial complexes. A simplicial complex that is vertex decomposable is shellable, hence Cohen--Macaulay. Through the Stanley--Reisner correspondence, vertex decompositions of simplicial complexes inform the study of squarefree monomial ideals, which we will be interested in studying in their capacity as the defining ideals of initial varieties of combinatorially-defined varieties. One limitation of this story is that a great deal of the combinatorial information defining a variety may be lost when one jumps in one fell swoop to its initial variety. A generalization of vertex decomposition, called geometric vertex decomposition, introduced by Knutson, Miller, and Yong in 2009, allows one to take smaller steps towards the initial variety, preserving more of the original combinatorial information along the way. In this talk, we will describe and give examples of geometric vertex decompositions in combinatorially-natural settings and discuss relationships among geometric vertex decomposition, various algebro-geometric invariants, and Frobenius splitting. We will end with some open problems.
This talk will touch on joint work with Andrew Berget, Emanuela De Negri, Elisa Gorla, Jenna Rajchgot, Lisa Seccia, Mayada Shahada, and Anna Weigandt.

proof_stages.label: Classical vertex decomposition and Stanley-Reisner model
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from vertex decompositions of pure simplicial complexes, translated via the Stanley-Reisner correspondence into squarefree monomial ideals, with link and deletion as the motivating operations.

proof_stages.label: Cohen-Macaulay payoffs
proof_stages.summary: Vertex decomposability is situated as a strong route to shellability and Cohen-Macaulayness, whose importance is explained through intersection theory, Hilbert functions, upper-bound phenomena, and mild singularities.

proof_stages.label: One-variable initial forms and algebraic link/deletion
proof_stages.summary: For an ideal and a chosen variable y, the speaker defines the initial y-form ideal, the colon ideal C_yI and deletion ideal N_yI, and gives the Grobner-basis/linearity criterion for the equality called a geometric vertex decomposition.

proof_stages.label: Recursive GVD and structural theorem
proof_stages.summary: The one-step construction is promoted to a recursive definition for unmixed ideals, with base cases analogous to simplexes and the main consequence that geometrically vertex decomposable ideals are radical and Cohen-Macaulay.

proof_stages.label: Determinantal and squarefree baselines
proof_stages.summary: The theory is checked against Stanley-Reisner ideals and generic determinantal ideals, showing that GVD recovers classical vertex decomposability and gives transparent link/deletion recursions for maximal and ladder determinantal ideals.

proof_stages.label: Schubert determinantal ideals and bumpless pipe dreams
proof_stages.summary: For matrix Schubert varieties, geometric vertex decomposition is related to droop moves on bumpless pipe dreams, allowing top-dimensional components and multidegrees to be read combinatorially while exposing limitations for K-theory.

proof_stages.label: Pairs of linear spaces and deletion-contraction
proof_stages.summary: A second combinatorial family, Schubert varieties of pairs of linear spaces, is described, where the GVD operations match deletion and contraction of the final column in the matrices realizing the data.

proof_stages.label: Frobenius splitting and open directions
proof_stages.summary: The final part connects geometric vertex decomposition to Frobenius splitting, including partial lifting results, and formulates open problems about K-theory, Reisner-type criteria, high embeddings, one-step Cohen-Macaulay degenerations, and singularities.
